title: The FAA's SMART System Is More Than an ATC Tool — It's Asking Airlines to Rethink How Dispatchers Work
summary: New details on SMART's September deployment reveal a "bounded introduction" at high altitude, a planned push of AI-generated reroutes directly to aircraft, and the extraordinary fact that airline operators are already telling the FAA it's moving too fast.
body_markdown:
A quick note to flag this is a follow-up to the post I published here on June 22 on the FAA's $875M SMART contract award to Air Space Intelligence. What's changed: Aviation Week reported additional deployment details this week — drawn from remarks by FAA Senior Certification Advisor Steve Fulton at the FAA/EASA annual safety conference on June 15 — that meaningfully shift how this story reads from a flight ops perspective.

## A "Bounded" Start That Still Has Big Implications

The SMART system's first operational demonstration, still targeted for this September, <cite index="78-7">will be a "bounded introduction," limited to air traffic at 24,000 feet and above.</cite> That framing is deliberately modest — the FAA is essentially telling the industry it's not flipping every switch at once. But the ambition underneath it is worth reading carefully.

<cite index="78-14,78-15">Fulton compared SMART to FAA's trajectory-based operations enhanced with AI and cloud computing "to sharpen predictions of where the airplanes will be in space and time." SMART is seen as further enhancement, not a human replacement.</cite> The goal, as Fulton described it, is to shift air traffic controllers into a role that looks more like modern flight deck management: <cite index="78-17,78-18">"Doing that for air traffic controllers has not been possible because we've kept our humans in a real-time, integration-of-information mode."</cite> That's a candid acknowledgment that the current system isn't so much broken as it is *architecturally stuck*. SMART is the attempt to unstick it.

From time spent on the vendor side of airline systems integration, I know how hard it is to change deeply embedded workflows — even when the technology is solid. The controller-side transition is one challenge. The airline-side implications are the part I think is getting underreported.

## What This Actually Requires From Airlines

<cite index="72-18,72-19,72-20">"We're using connectivity with the aircraft through a variety of options," Fulton said. "We can push through airborne reroute but we're respecting the authority of the controller. But those are the types of details we're working out with our industry partners, how they want us to connect to their airplanes."</cite>

That sentence deserves more attention than it's getting. SMART isn't just a tool that runs quietly in an FAA data center. If it's generating reroutes and pushing them toward aircraft, then airline Operations Control Centers are going to need to receive, evaluate, and act on that data stream — in a structured, consistent way. That's a dispatch workflow question as much as an ATC question. Airlines will need to think about how SMART's outputs interact with their own flight planning systems, their existing reroute procedures, and how dispatchers prioritize AI-generated recommendations from two directions: their own internal tools and now a government system.

<cite index="86-13">SMART is designed to help "the FAA, airlines, and operators align on the most efficient routes and departure and arrival times, improving efficiency, predictability, and system-wide performance."</cite> That's a collaborative data model — but collaboration requires integration, and integration requires readiness on both sides of the transaction.

## The Pace Question

Perhaps the most telling detail in this week's reporting: <cite index="78-9,78-10">Fulton acknowledged some stakeholders are concerned the FAA's pace is too brisk. "It's not often you hear industry say to the FAA, 'You're going too fast,' but we're getting that from our operator partners as we're working with them to be able to deploy this in September."</cite>

That's not a small admission. Airlines and aviation operators typically push regulators to move faster, not slower. When you hear the reverse, it usually means the integration work hasn't caught up with the procurement announcement — and that the September target is creating real pressure on the operations teams responsible for making this work. <cite index="80-3">ASI has said both technologies will be fully deployed in the next 24 months,</cite> so the September demonstration is explicitly a validation step, not a full rollout. But the gap between a "bounded introduction" and production-grade integration across dozens of airline OCCs is where the real work will happen.

I'll be watching closely for how airlines — particularly the larger U.S. carriers who were clearly in those operator consultations — describe their readiness when September gets closer. The technology story is already written. The workflow story is just getting started.

## Sources

- [Aviation Week — FAA Expands On New ATC Software Deployment Details](https://aviationweek.com/aerospace/emerging-technologies/faa-expands-new-atc-software-deployment-details)
- [AVweb — FAA SMART Airspace Tool Could Demo In September](https://avweb.com/aviation-news/faa-smart-airspace-tool-could-launch-sept/)
- [FedScoop — FAA brings on AI, software partner for ATC modernization](https://fedscoop.com/faa-atc-modernization-tech-upgrade-uas/)
- [FAA Newsroom — Modern Skies: Air Space Intelligence Contract Announcement](https://www.faa.gov/newsroom/modern-skies-trumps-transportation-secretary-sean-p-duffy-selects-air-space-intelligence)
questions: When airline operators tell the FAA 'you're going too fast,' in your experience does that usually reflect a genuine integration problem, or is it more of a negotiating posture to buy more time? (Genuine integration concern / Mostly posturing / Both, depending on the operator)

The post frames SMART as creating a new data stream that OCC dispatchers will need to handle alongside their own tools. From your time at the dispatch/flight ops software side of the industry, is that a manageable incremental workflow change — or does it require something closer to a system redesign at the airline level?

The FAA's analogy here is essentially: SMART does for controllers what modern flight deck automation did for pilots — moves them from real-time integrators to supervisors. Do you think that analogy holds up, or is the ATC environment different enough that it breaks down?
